topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished mahogany of Dr. Vance’s office reflected the harsh overhead lights, making the silence feel thick and suffocating. I tapped a restless rhythm against my thigh, the sound sharp in the quiet room. It had been three months since I’d walked in, seeking clarity on a recurring anxiety that had been hijacking my work. And there he was, sitting behind the expansive desk, his posture impeccably calm, his eyes holding a disconcerting, knowing warmth. It was Mark. The boy who used to dismantle my confidence with casual cruelty in the hallways of middle school. Now, he wore a crisp white coat, and the sneer had been replaced by an almost unnerving empathy. "So," he began, his voice a low, measured baritone, "tell me about the pressure you feel when you try to keep everyone else happy." I stared at the water glass, my jaw tight. I wanted to lash out, to demand he just stop analyzing me, but the words caught in my throat. I just wanted him to stop looking at me like I was some fascinating, broken specimen under glass.

[story 2]

Every time I tried to articulate the knot tightening in my chest, the words felt clumsy, inadequate. I paced the length of the waiting room, my shoes squeaking on the linoleum, my shoulders hiked up toward my ears. This session was proving to be an exercise in pure aggravation. Mark, the architect of my adolescent misery, was now dissecting my self-doubt with surgical precision. He leaned forward, resting his elbows on the desk, his expression one of deep, patient concern. "It seems," he observed, his gaze steady and unwavering, "that the fear of inadequacy is rooted in a very specific set of early experiences." I stopped pacing abruptly, planting my hands on my hips. A sharp, involuntary exhale escaped me. "I don't need an analysis," I managed, my voice pitched higher than intended. "I need solutions. I need someone to tell me how to *fix* this, not just map out the damage." He simply smiled, a small, almost pitying curve of his lips. "Perhaps," he suggested softly, "the solution lies in understanding the damage first."

[story 3]

I kept catching myself glaring at the framed photo on his bookshelf—a grainy picture of a younger Mark, all sharp angles and barely concealed arrogance. The sheer absurdity of the situation churned in my gut. This man, who used to relish the sting of my humiliation, was now gently guiding me through the wreckage of my past. I felt a surge of hot irritation, a desperate need to shatter the veneer of his professional composure. "Look," I snapped, leaning across the consultation table, my voice laced with steel, "I appreciate the insight, truly. But this isn't a textbook case. This is *my* life, and I don't need a retrospective on my childhood trauma delivered by the person who used to mock my bad handwriting." Mark didn't flinch. He merely steepled his fingers, his expression unreadable, radiating a quiet, almost infuriating patience. "And what, precisely, do you believe would be more helpful than understanding the root cause?" he inquired, his tone suggesting I was arguing with a particularly stubborn child. I slumped back into my chair, the energy draining out of me, leaving a heavy, leaden sensation in my limbs.
